text:
“It was so scary,” the Olympic gymnast said.

Since her retirement, Olympic gymnastAly Raismanhas been hospitalized twice for“stroke-like symptoms,”she recently told Alex Cooper on theCall Her Daddypodcast. In the interview, Raisman recounted the experiences and attributed them both to trauma and stress.

“If you don’t pick a day to rest, your body will pick it for you,”—that quote, Raisman said,  “encapsulates” her episodes. As a baseline, she tends to have “different minor issues” with her health “depending on what I’m going through at the time,” she said, but there were two “intense” events during which she was reviewed for stroke-like symptoms. “I can’t remember my name. I’m slurring my words. I can barely speak. Both times I was tested for a stroke because I literally couldn’t move my body,” she recalled.

If you’re not familiar, the symptoms of astroke, according to theNational Heart, Blood, and Lung Institute, are:

Raisman said it was all “so scary,” a fear that was exacerbated by the PTSD she lives with as a result of the abuse she experienced in medical settings as a young gymnast. In 2018, U.S. gymnastics doctor Larry Nassarwas sentenced to up to 175 years in prisonfor sexually abusing his patients, among other sex crimes. Over 150 women, including Raisman, came forward as victims.

Raisman’s past made her understandably wary when it came to being alone with medics during her recent health scares. “The first time it happened was during COVID. And so, they wouldn’t let my mom in the ambulance with me,” she recalled. “I was aware enough to know, ‘Oh my God, I have two men [with me], I can’t move my arms and my legs, I can’t move my body, I can’t speak. What if they take advantage of me?’”

“I was really still struggling a lot with PTSD,” she added. “People don’t realize how much it still lives with you when you’ve been through something traumatic. So, that was really hard for me.”

The second episode happened a year and a half later, Raisman explained, and struck her with “complete body paralysis” that left her hospitalized for three days. “They wouldn’t release me because I couldn’t sit up on my own. It took me so long. I needed help walking, going to the bathroom,” she explained. “It was the most [difficult] to be able to go from being an athlete and being able to push myself so much to literally not even be able to move my fingers, move my legs.”

In the medical community, bouts of stroke-like symptoms are known as “stroke mimics,”  and they’ve been linked to a series of potential causes including migraines, vertigo, and, commonly, functional neurological disorder (FND), per theAmerican Heart Association (AHA), which is a condition that can affect sensation, movement, speech, and vision. PTSD symptomsare commonin people with FNDs, and researchers think PTSD could even be a cause of them. A recentstudyfound that people who have stroke mimics are susceptible to developing (or developing worsened) PTSD.

“I wonder what is the chicken and what is the egg here?” Natalia Rost, M.D., chief of the stroke division at Massachusetts General Hospital in Boston told the AHA. “Folks who are vulnerable to psychological stress can experience stroke mimics, which could be the strongest connection to developing PTSD after a stroke mimic.”

With so much unknown about stroke mimics, Raisman said she was initially “ignored” by doctors. “It’s appalling,” she said. “It wasn’t until they realized that I was a very high-level athlete, that they were very nice to me and taking care of me, which is also triggering for me because I already have trust issues with doctors.”

Sinceher retirement from gymnastics in 2020, she has been focusing on her personal life. “I’ve been doing therapy for many years, and I think therapy can be so impactful and so helpful,” she toldWondermindin 2022. “I’m really just trying to remember that it’s important for me to just stick with it, be patient, communicate with my therapist, my friends, and family. So I’m just kind of taking it day by day.”

During the upcoming Paris Summer Games, Raisman will be an official hospitality ambassador alongside Michael Phelps, perNBC, and she’s curious to see how her body will react. “I’m gonna be so busy,” she told Cooper. “There’s gonna be a lot of triggers. I might see someone that maybe didn’t protect me in the past. So, it’s working on that. It’s very complicated.”

title: Aly Raisman Was Hospitalized Twice for ‘Stroke-Like Symptoms’
description: Olympic gymnast Aly Raisman was hospitalized twice with “stroke-like symptoms” linked to stress and trauma. She called the experience “so scary.”
